title: 2025 12 10 - HE
displayTitle: 2025-12-10 - Health Committee - Briefing from the New Zealand Drug Foundation on methamphetamine consumption
topic: Briefing from the New Zealand Drug Foundation on methamphetamine consumption
description: Wednesday, 10th December 2025 | Health Committee Briefing from the New Zealand Drug Foundation on methamphetamine consumption Hearing of evidence: New Zealand Drug Foundation Sarah Helm, Executive Director
descriptionSummary: Briefing from the New Zealand Drug Foundation on methamphetamine consumption. New Zealand Drug Foundation. Sarah Helm, Executive Director

chunks.text: Okay. Hey, Sarah, thank you very much for joining the committee today. Uh, very good to see you. Uh, we've got members around the, round the room here today. Um, we have you for 10 minutes so, um, if you want to leave us some time for questions that would be helpful.

But ultimately it's, it's your time so I will hand that to you now. Thank you. Oh, Kia ora. Thank you so much, Sam. Um, so I'll just, uh, give about a five-minute talk. Hopefully I can keep it to that and then time for questions. Um, and I'll run through a little bit of, sort of, uh, more basic information and then get into more what we do to address methamphetamine harm in the community.

So, um, thank you very much for having me. It's a very, very important topic. I think, um, particularly important because as you know methamphetamine consumption has doubled in the country in that, um, huge increase has been sustained for something like 18 months at this point. Um, so we don't imagine that's going away anytime soon. Um, so this is something that it's really good for Parliament to understand and be able to grapple with, um, as time m- marches on. We've been really pleased to see some announcements of some health interventions, um, from government which we have

applauded, um, more recently. Um, so, um, methamphetamine is a stimulant, um, and it's in a group of substances called amphetamines, um, like speed, uh, which was around in the '90s in particular. Um, it's snorted, injected, smoked, and swallowed, and there are different harms that come with the different types of routes of administration, as we call it. Um, swallowing, for example, can help to avoid some of the harms if it's very carefully dosed.

Um, and different, uh, routes of administration, like injecting, can come with communicable disease and things like, uh, sterile injecting equipment through the needle exchangers, uh, help to counter some of those harms. Um, we can have more acute harms that happen, uh, when people are taking a lot, like a type of overdose that we call over-amping where the person might experience seizures, um, uh, a racing heart, chest pain, um, a high temperature, and those kinds of things, or psychosis. Um, and more chronic harms are things

like it being a cardiotoxic substance and resulting in cardiovascular issues. Uh, smoke- smoking can result in tooth decay and so forth. Um, we have a really long history with amphetamine use in New Zealand. Um, in the 1960s it was available, amphetamines were available from pharmacies. Um, I understand, uh, sometimes it was given on flights as a little pick-me-up as you arrived (laughs) into, uh, different countries. Um, and it became very popular in the 1990s as some as you- some of you will recall.

Um, it was being manufactured with cold and flu medication at the time. Then when pseudoephedrine was banned, it was replaced with imported produc- imported products. Um, the reason for the consumption having doubled we think is multifaceted. So, um, the price has come down. Uh, that's probably linked to changes in our organized crime environment and an increase in the international supply.

And certainly we're seeing more methamphetamine in other countries that haven't experienced as much methamphetamine in their drug supply. On top of that, methamphetamine has, um, a hunger suppressant effect. Um, so it can also be, uh, people might seek it out to suppress hunger or, um, deal with some hardship that they might be experiencing, um, like being cold and being outdoors. Um, and about 1% of the population use methamphetamine.

In the most recent New Zealand, uh, Ministry of Health data, that 1% figure was actually confirmed again. What we do think is that people, uh... So that increased consumption, so it's measured through the wastewater. And wastewater you sometimes hear a bit of misinformation about. So the wastewater picks up, um, methamphetamine that's been metabolized, i.e.

it's been through the human body so it's not a product that's been dumped down the toilet, if you like. Um, and, um, it does exclude septic tanks but it's a very, very good measure of our total volume of methamphetamine consumed in the country. So, the most recent, um, New Zealand Drug Trend survey information we have, um, does show a very big increase in the frequency of use among some people.

So most people who use meth use it pretty infrequently, but we've seen a growth in the, um, frequency of use, uh, with, worryingly, we've gone from 19% using it, um, daily or almost daily back in 2022, '23, up to 29%. So we've had like a 10% increase, or 10% of the total population who use methamphetamine now using it daily.

Um, so, um, that's some of the background. Um, and I think it's important to note that not everybody who uses methamphetamine actually n- needs addiction treatment. So most people use it very infrequently. And one of the things we've been trying to encourage is more support along the spectrum of use, both with harm reduction and other types of more accessible types of treatment.

So we've been talking about mutual aid. So mutual aid, most famously you'll be aware of Narcotics Anonymous or, um, alcohol A- AA meetings. Um, there's some other types of, um, that kind of community-led, uh-... the kind of supports that can happen much more, uh, early in somebody's trajectory, doesn't require them to submit to a higher power or, um, decide that they're going to be completely abstinent from the get-go. That could be much more useful in terms of helping to, uh, people to manage their use much more earlier in their, uh,

equation. One of the, um, things about methamphetamine is the reasons people use it. So people use it to, uh, enable them to work really long hours. Uh, they might use it as a party substance, um, which might not be how people perceive it's being used. Um, it can be because of the nature of the effects of methamphetamine, it can be helpful from, uh, for people who are using it for sexual, uh, you know, uh, sex work or for, um, the chemsex community.

Um, it also helps to suppress hunger, as I mentioned. And for people who use ADHD, so we're increasingly aware of that, um, it can also be, um, used as a form of self-medication. Not recommending that, of course. Um, but actually we are really aware that people do use it very knowingly for self-medication. A very recent University of Queensland, um, research, um, which is their sort of drug trends research, um, they've asked, um, their cohort of people who use illicit stimulants and people who inject drugs, so two different cohorts but there's

crossover there, um, and they found that over half of them met the criteria for the DSM for ADHD. Um, very few of them were diagnosed. Um, an even smaller group were actually being treated, but a huge number of them were reporting that they were self-medicating using illicits- illicitly acquired medications or substances. So some of what we think would help, um, is, um, on the supply side, so we've invested very heavily in the policing and supply seizure stuff.

And on the one hand, it feels, um, really efficacious but on another hand- on another hand, it has its limitations. So first of all, it can be very short-lived, so Operation High Water in Opotiki that you might remember was in the news late last year, so in November 2024, the police managed to get methamphetamine consumption approximately halved but by April, we were back at similar levels as the pre-seizure. Now that amount is bouncing around, um, and I understand that police are working with the wider system to try and

marry up the seizures with the, um, demand reduction activities but there are some limitations and it's very expensive. So we're very quick to put money into that side of things but very reticent to put it on the health side of things, which is much more long term, uh, ef- ef- effective change. The other thing is sometimes there can be unintended consequences of the seizures, so usually the drug will be replaced, whether it's replaced with the same drug or a different drug can be really problematic as well so there can be an unintended

outcome of overdoses and other problems. So some of the demand side interventions that we think, uh, would be really helpful and some of this has been, um, covered by the government's recent package so we're very happy about that, is just increasing the support for substance use disorder treatment, so addiction treatment, harm reduction, um, the types of help so mutual aid and more support along the spectrum of use. Um, not waiting for somebody to be in jail or to have addiction before offering them help and that's particularly

important in this particular, um, issue because people who are long term substance use disorder, um, they're often then selling some of the product in order for them to be able to support their habits. You're actually propping up the supply when you fail to address the demand. Um, looking at the ADHD component, um, looking at Te Ara Oranga, some of Te Ara Oranga's approaches up north have been copied by this new package so the government have announced.

We're, we're almost at time, Sarah, just- Yeah. ... FYI. Medically assisted treatment, um, there's some really new evidence coming through on medically assisted treatment so stimulant supply, uh, st- st- stimulant substitution therapy or even psilocybin, there's emerging evidence of, um, and stigma, reducing stigma so that people actually come forward for help and I'll stop talking. Thank you very much.

Thank you. Almost perfect at 10 minutes- (laughs)... but I'll go to Ingrid. Thank you, Sarah, for the, um, great evidenced-based work that you and your organization do and for your advocacy. Um, I'm really interested in your commentary around supply and demand side, um, and the balance of that and I note the Helen Clark Foundation has recently showed that there is a, an appetite for more funding to go into supporting people on the demand side. I guess if we're looking at what the barriers are, uh, it seems to me that workforce but also referrals through GPs to mental

health services, um, could be inhibitors even if we were able to recalibrate some of the spending. Do you have some commentary on that and some potential solutions that could help to get more support on the, on the supply side? Sorry, the demand side. And I'll just say that w- to be, um, we're over time so if we can be- Okay, I'll keep it short.

... to the point would be great. Thank you. Thank you. Um, I think a couple of things. Not everything has to be, um, uh, so you can have peer-led interventions like mutual aid that will help to add capacity. You've also seen some positive announcements on workforce that have come from government more recently. Um, so there'll be 15 new, um, people being trained as I understand it from the workforce package as part of that methamphetamine package.

I think it's good to bring new people in so there's a sort of approach being taken that is learn as you learn, which we think is fantastic. One of the other components is retention of the workforce. So the workforce is leaving in droves so we need to address the retention issues. That probably looks like pay but it might also look like finding out, uh, why they're leaving and addressing some of those issues.

Thank you, Sarah. Thank you very much for, um, making yourself available to the committee today. We greatly appreciate your time. That's all right. Thank you so much. And all your hard work too. Thank you. You too. Have a good New Year's and Christmas. Kia ora. Likewise. Bye.
